content.text: is a lightweight Python web framework built on two core libraries:
(the WSGI toolkit that handles HTTP) and

(the templating engine). Pallets Projects maintains it and it ships with almost nothing opinionated out of the box — no ORM, no form library, no authentication system. You add exactly what you need through extensions or by wiring in third-party libraries directly. This philosophy is sometimes called a micro-framework or, more precisely, an un-opinionated framework: Flask provides the plumbing for routing and request/response handling but leaves every architectural decision to you.

Most web frameworks bundle decisions along with functionality: Django ships an ORM, an admin panel, a migration engine, and a forms library because it assumes a full-featured server-side application. Flask makes the opposite trade. Its codebase is small enough to read in a day, its extension API is minimal, and adding a database, auth layer, or serialiser is always an explicit import rather than a hidden default. That explicitness matters for agentic AI projects, where the web tier is typically a thin HTTP adapter over an agent loop rather than a feature-rich application in its own right. Flask lets you expose endpoints quickly without inheriting dependencies you do not need.
The core framework is under 4 000 lines of Python. There is little hidden behaviour to debug and the execution path from a request arriving to your code running is short and traceable.
A working API endpoint is three lines of Python. The built-in development server with live reloading speeds up iteration.

You choose the ORM (SQLAlchemy, Peewee, raw psycopg), the auth strategy (JWT, session, API key), and the serialiser (marshmallow, Pydantic, or plain dicts). No framework assumption can conflict with your requirements.

Flask's Blueprint system lets you split a large application into independently developed modules, each with its own routes, error handlers, and static files, all registered on the core app at startup.
Flask has been in production since 2010. Extensions for SQLAlchemy, migrations, JWT, caching, rate-limiting, CORS, and testing are stable and widely adopted.
The Flask test client creates a real request context without a running server, making unit and integration tests fast and free of network side effects.
Flask 2.0+ supports async view functions. Async is per-route, not a global rewrite of the application, so you adopt it only where it helps.
Freedom is also work. A Django project starts with auth, ORM, and admin wired together. A Flask project requires you to pick and integrate each piece deliberately.

are proxy objects tied to the active request context. Code that runs outside a request context — background threads, CLI commands, Celery tasks — must push an application context manually or it will raise
RuntimeError: Working outside of application context

Each request blocks a Gunicorn worker thread. For workloads with heavy I/O concurrency — streaming LLM responses, many parallel external calls, WebSocket connections — FastAPI or a native ASGI framework fits better.
The Flask extension ecosystem is large but not uniformly maintained. Always check a package's GitHub activity and release history before depending on it in production.
Teams building a large monolith with complex admin requirements often outgrow Flask. Django's built-ins save substantial time at that scale.

When a client sends an HTTP request, the WSGI server (Gunicorn, uWSGI, or Waitress in production; Flask's built-in server in development) receives the raw TCP data, parses it into a dictionary of environment variables, and calls the Flask application object as a callable:
. Flask then does the following in sequence:
available to all code in the call chain.
hooks — common uses include authentication checks, logging, and rate limiting.
Dispatches to the matching view function based on the URL map built from all
The view function returns a value — a string, a dict, a
Flask converts the return value into an HTTP response and hands it back to the WSGI server.
WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface, PEP 3333) is the standard Python interface between web servers and applications. Any WSGI server can host any WSGI application. Flask implements

on its application class, making the entire framework a single callable. This means you can mount a Flask app as a sub-application inside another WSGI middleware chain, or wrap it with custom middleware that inspects or transforms requests before Flask ever sees them.

instances — thread-local references that always point at the object on top of the current thread's context stack. Multiple concurrent requests running in different threads can all import the same module-level names without sharing state. The downside is that these proxies are only valid while a request or application context is active.

The diagram below shows the complete request/response cycle, from the client through the WSGI server, Flask's routing and Blueprint dispatch, the view function, and back to the client. The lower panel lists the most common Flask extensions that plug into this cycle.

attrs.alt: Flask request/response cycle: Client → WSGI Server (Gunicorn/uWSGI) → Flask App (middleware, URL routing) → Blueprint → View Function → Response (Jinja2 HTML or JSON) → Client

content.text: When the web tier wraps business logic that lives elsewhere — an agent orchestrator, an LLM pipeline, a data-processing service — Flask's small footprint keeps the API layer from dominating the codebase.
Each Flask service is self-contained. You can run ten Flask apps in ten containers, each with its own dependencies, without the overhead of a full-featured framework per service.
Flask is a good first choice when requirements are unclear and you need to iterate quickly on the API shape before committing to a schema or ORM model.
If the team prefers to choose their own ORM, auth library, and serialiser rather than inherit framework defaults, Flask's integration model is less surprising.

the application has complex server-side HTML with admin panels (Django wins there), requires high-concurrency streaming (FastAPI/ASGI is a better fit), or the team wants strong conventions from day one.

pip install flask

# For production deployment
pip install flask gunicorn

# Common extensions
pip install flask-sqlalchemy flask-migrate flask-jwt-extended flask-cors
A small Flask project can live in a single file. Once it grows past a few dozen routes, the app factory pattern with Blueprints is the standard way to organise it:

myproject/
  app/
    __init__.py       # create_app() factory
    config.py         # environment-specific config classes
    auth/
      __init__.py
      routes.py       # Blueprint: auth_bp
    items/
      __init__.py
      routes.py       # Blueprint: items_bp
    models.py         # SQLAlchemy models
  wsgi.py             # production entry point
  tests/
    conftest.py       # pytest fixtures: app, client
    test_items.py
Example 1: JSON REST API with routes and error handling

The example below covers the most common Flask patterns: a parameterised route, query-parameter extraction, a POST endpoint that reads a JSON body, and custom error handlers that return consistent JSON error shapes regardless of the error origin.
Example 2: Blueprints for modular application structure

Blueprints let you split a growing application into domain-focused modules. Each Blueprint defines its own URL prefix, view functions, and error handlers, then is registered on the core application object at startup. The URL map is merged at registration time — all routing still happens in a single table.
Example 3: App factory pattern and configuration

The app factory pattern — a function that constructs and returns the Flask app — solves two problems: it makes the application configurable by environment (development versus production), and it prevents extension state from leaking between test cases when each test creates a fresh app instance.

# config.py
import os

class BaseConfig:
    SECRET_KEY = os.environ.get('SECRET_KEY', 'dev-secret-change-me')
    SQLALCHEMY_TRACK_MODIFICATIONS = False

class DevelopmentConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEBUG = True
    SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI = 'sqlite:///dev.db'

class ProductionConfig(BaseConfig):
    DEBUG = False
    SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI = os.environ['DATABASE_URL']

config_map = {
    'development': DevelopmentConfig,
    'production': ProductionConfig,
}


# app/__init__.py (app factory)
from flask import Flask
from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy
from .config import config_map

db = SQLAlchemy()  # extension instance created outside create_app

def create_app(env: str = 'development') -> Flask:
    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.config.from_object(config_map[env])

    # Initialise extensions with the app instance
    db.init_app(app)

    # Register blueprints
    from .auth.routes import auth_bp
    from .items.routes import items_bp
    app.register_blueprint(auth_bp)
    app.register_blueprint(items_bp)

    return app


# wsgi.py (entry point for production)
from app import create_app

app = create_app('production')

# gunicorn wsgi:app
# flask --app wsgi run   (development)

A common pattern in agentic AI projects is a Flask API that receives webhook calls or user prompts, enqueues them to a Celery task queue, and returns a job ID for the client to poll. The Flask layer handles HTTP plumbing: request validation, authentication via

, and serialisation. The agent logic lives entirely in the Celery task, which calls the LLM and stores results in a database. Flask is kept thin and stateless — it does not hold any agent state itself, it only translates between HTTP and the task queue. This separation means you can scale the Flask API tier and the worker tier independently: add more Gunicorn workers behind an Nginx reverse proxy for API throughput, add more Celery workers for LLM concurrency.

The same pattern applies to building a local tool server for an MCP (Model Context Protocol) host. You define one Flask route per tool, each route validates arguments, calls the underlying function, and returns a JSON-serialised result. Flask's test client then makes it straightforward to write integration tests that exercise the full tool-handling path without a running HTTP server, keeping the test suite fast even as the number of tools grows.
Using the development server in production:

Flask's built-in server is single-threaded and has no production hardening. Always run behind Gunicorn, uWSGI, or Waitress in production. The app prints a warning if you forget, but that warning is easy to miss in Docker logs.
Moving logic into a helper module and calling it from a Celery task or a CLI command raises
RuntimeError: Working outside of request context
. Pass the data you need as function arguments, or push an application context manually with
Mutable default state on the app object:

Flask does not validate incoming JSON against a schema. Production endpoints should validate with Pydantic, marshmallow, or manually before touching the data. Silently ignoring unexpected fields or missing keys leads to obscure errors downstream.
Long-running work inside a view function:
A view that takes ten seconds blocks a Gunicorn worker for ten seconds. Off-load to Celery, a thread pool, or an async background task and return 202 Accepted with a task ID immediately.
Flask's session cookie is signed with
. Leaving it at the development default in production lets anyone forge sessions.
Always use the app factory pattern:
It enables environment-specific config, clean test isolation, and deferred extension initialisation. The single-file style is fine for a tutorial but creates tight coupling in production code.

View functions should do three things: extract inputs from the request, call a service or domain function, and return a response. Business logic belongs in a separate layer that does not import Flask.
for at least 400, 404, and 500 and return a consistent JSON shape. Clients should never receive raw HTML error pages from a JSON API.
or database credentials. Use python-dotenv for local development and a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) in production.
Flask extensions often have breaking changes between minor versions. Pin all dependencies in
Use Flask's test client in tests:
Create the app with the test config and use
. Avoid starting a real HTTP server for unit or integration tests — the test client is faster and side-effect-free.

Flask's synchronous request handling means each Gunicorn worker handles one request at a time. For CPU-bound endpoints this is optimal — workers use all available CPU without context-switch overhead. For I/O-bound endpoints (database queries, LLM calls, external HTTP), the conventional solution is to run more workers:
runs four worker processes, each handling one request simultaneously. A common starting point is
For endpoints that make many concurrent I/O calls — for example, fanning out to five external APIs per request — the synchronous model wastes threads. Options are:

patches Python I/O primitives with cooperative greenlets. Each worker handles many concurrent requests without real threads. Flask code needs no changes, but libraries that use C extensions for I/O may not be gevent-compatible.
and Flask 2.0+ will await it. Use this for individual endpoints that await multiple I/O calls — it does not make the entire app non-blocking.
Enqueue long tasks to Celery and return a polling URL immediately. The Flask tier stays fast and stateless regardless of task duration.
Flask-Caching with Redis cuts repeated database round-trips. Cache at the view level with
or at the data-access layer for finer granularity.

Flask itself adds negligible overhead: the router, context push/pop, and Jinja2 render typically consume fewer than one millisecond per request on commodity hardware. Bottlenecks are almost always in the database or external service calls, not in the framework.
Integrates SQLAlchemy ORM with the application context so the database session is tied to the request lifecycle and automatically cleaned up after each request.
Alembic-based schema migrations managed from the Flask CLI:
JWT creation, refresh, and verification with decorators (
) and helpers for access and refresh token management.
Redis, Memcached, or in-process cache with a clean decorator API and a pluggable backend.
Sets CORS headers per-route or globally. Required when the API is called from a browser on a different origin.
Not a Flask extension but integrates cleanly. Shares the Flask app context inside tasks, giving access to
Schema validation and serialisation libraries commonly used with Flask to validate request bodies and serialise response objects into consistent shapes.
fixture creates the app and returns a test client; individual tests call
Flask's session cookie is signed with
. A key leaked or guessed lets attackers forge sessions. Rotate the key by setting a new environment variable and redeploying; existing sessions are invalidated immediately.
Flask does not include CSRF protection. For HTML forms, use Flask-WTF. For JSON APIs authenticated via Bearer tokens rather than cookies, CSRF is generally not a concern.
Always use the ORM's parameterised queries or
with bound parameters. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
Validate all inputs — type, length, and allowed values — before they reach business logic. Pydantic model parsing or marshmallow strict schemas are the idiomatic choices.
Use Flask-Limiter (backed by Redis) to prevent brute-force attacks on authentication endpoints and abuse of expensive AI-powered endpoints.
Deploy behind Nginx or an ALB. Nginx handles TLS termination, request buffering, and static file serving. Set Werkzeug's
middleware so Flask reads the correct client IP from
or configure Dependabot in CI to catch known CVEs in Flask, Werkzeug, and extensions before they reach production.
request router with a managed lifecycle
you are registering an entry in a URL map. When a request arrives, Flask looks up the map, creates contexts so that global-looking names like

resolve to the current request, calls your function, and tears the context down. Everything else — validation, auth, databases, caching — is your responsibility, and you plug it in by importing and configuring it during application startup (

). The framework never does anything you did not ask for. If something unexpected is happening, it is coming from an extension you installed, a middleware in the chain, or your own before/after hooks — not from hidden framework machinery.
Blueprints are namespaced route groups. They let you organise the URL map into logical modules without changing how requests are processed. An
are both merged into the same URL map on
. There is no separate router or sub-application — just a merged map and a shared request lifecycle.
— it handles HTTP routing and the request/response lifecycle, and nothing more by default. Every other capability is an explicit addition.
Every request creates and tears down an
are only valid inside these contexts.
organise routes into modules with URL prefixes; the
) enables environment-specific config and clean test isolation between test cases.
Flask is synchronous by default. Scale I/O-bound applications with multiple Gunicorn workers, Gevent workers, or by off-loading to a task queue — not by rewriting everything as async.
Keep view functions thin: extract inputs, call a service, return a response. Business logic must not import Flask so it can be tested and reused outside the HTTP context.
Always run Flask behind a production WSGI server (Gunicorn, uWSGI, Waitress). The built-in development server is single-threaded and unsuitable for real traffic.
For agentic AI applications, Flask excels as a
— exposing agent capabilities as REST endpoints while keeping the agent logic completely independent of the web framework.
